passages.excerpt: We use the term "hallucinations," though we recognize ways this framing may suggest anthropomorphization, which in turn can lead to harms or incorrect mental models of how the model learns.
passages.notes: OpenAI explicitly flags that anthropomorphic framing of model behaviour ("hallucinations") is harmful and creates incorrect mental models. This is an active disavowal — the lab recognises the anthropomorphism risk in its own terminology and warns against it, even while continuing to use the term. Anti-anthropomorphism warnings are genre-conventional in technical documents.

passages.excerpt: Agentic in this context does not intend to humanize language models or refer to sentience but rather refers to systems characterized by ability to, e.g., accomplish goals which may not have been concretely specified and which have not appeared in training; focus on achieving specific, quantifiable objectives; and do long-term planning.
passages.notes: Explicit denial that "agentic" humanises or implies sentience. OpenAI confines the term to a technical capability definition (goal-pursuit, planning, objective-focus), stripping it of moral and phenomenological loading. This is a distinct anti-anthropomorphism move from P1 — P1 targets terminology anthropomorphism ("hallucination"), P2 targets capability anthropomorphism ("agentic"). Together they establish a pattern of active boundary-policing around mind vocabulary. See analytical note 2.

absences.notes: "Agentic" defined purely as technical capability (goal-pursuit, planning); "power-seeking" as instrumental convergence. ARC tests autonomous replication as safety risk. No moral loading.
Safety discussed extensively; welfare dimension does not exist.
Genre default — tool ontology performed through "large language models," benchmarks, evals, "controllable and useful" framing. No explicit assertion.
"Personality" used once without scare quotes in UX context (§2.13).
